agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of the Shipment aggregate from a 1:1 Shipment-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental domain model change affecting the core bounded context of shipment fulfillment, with downstream effects on data migration, external integrations, and delivery operations.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods being transported from an origin to one or more delivery destinations. The root aggregate that owns Recipients."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"A party designated to receive part or all of a Shipment. Has a name, address, phone number, and delivery preferences."},{"term":"Delivery Preferences","definition":"Recipient-specific instructions governing how and when delivery should occur (e.g., time windows, safe-place authorization, signature requirements)."},{"term":"Primary Recipient","definition":"The first or default Recipient on a Shipment. For migrated legacy data, this is the original single Recipient. Required on every Shipment to preserve the invariant that a Shipment must have at least one Recipient."},{"term":"Multi-Recipient Shipment","definition":"A Shipment with two or more Recipients, each potentially at a different address with different delivery preferences."},{"term":"Split Delivery","definition":"The operational outcome of a multi-recipient Shipment where the courier must make multiple stops to fulfill a single Shipment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment MUST have at least one Recipient at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment without a Recipient has no delivery target and is physically undeliverable. The system is in a corrupt state if this is violated.","testable_assertion":"COUNT(shipment.recipients) >= 1 for every Shipment in any state."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Each Recipient within a Shipment MUST have a unique address. Two Recipients at the same address on the same Shipment is semantically invalid.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Duplicate addresses on the same Shipment indicate data corruption or a UI error—the business has no concept of delivering twice to the same address under one Shipment.","testable_assertion":"No two Recipients on the same Shipment share the same normalized address."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Each Recipient MUST have a name, address, and phone number. Delivery preferences are optional and default to standard delivery if not specified.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Name, address, and phone are the minimum required to attempt delivery and to contact the Recipient. Without any one of these, the courier cannot fulfill the delivery.","testable_assertion":"For every Recipient: name IS NOT NULL, address IS NOT NULL, phone IS NOT NULL."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Legacy single-recipient Shipments must be migrated to the new model by wrapping the existing Recipient as the sole entry in a Recipients collection, preserving all existing data.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a migration policy, not a state integrity constraint. The approach could change (e.g., lazy migration vs. batch).","testable_assertion":"After migration, every legacy Shipment has exactly one Recipient in its collection, and the Recipient's name, address, and phone match the original values."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"External partner APIs MUST continue to receive single-recipient payloads for single-recipient Shipments. Multi-recipient Shipments must be projected as N separate partner notifications or a new versioned payload, per partner agreement.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an integration policy that can be renegotiated with partners. It is not a domain integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Partner API calls for single-recipient Shipments produce payloads identical in structure to the current format. Multi-recipient Shipments produce either N payloads or a v2 payload, never an invalid hybrid."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"Delivery status must be tracked per Recipient, not per Shipment. A Shipment is complete only when ALL Recipients have received delivery.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If delivery status remains at the Shipment level, multi-recipient Shipments cannot accurately represent partial delivery states—this would corrupt operational truth.","testable_assertion":"Shipment.status == DELIVERED if and only if ALL Recipient.status == DELIVERED."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"Delivery preferences are scoped to each Recipient individually and do not cascade or inherit from the Shipment or other Recipients.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Recipient A wanting signature-required and Recipient B wanting safe-place are independent constraints. Merging or inheriting preferences would violate delivery instructions.","testable_assertion":"Modifying Recipient A's delivery preferences does not change Recipient B's delivery preferences on the same Shipment."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"The user adds one or more Recipients with name, address, phone, and optional delivery preferences","then":"The Shipment is created with a Recipients collection containing all specified Recipients, each with their individual preferences"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Shipment exists with two Recipients","when":"Delivery is completed for Recipient A but not Recipient B","then":"The Shipment status is PARTIALLY_DELIVERED and Recipient A's status is DELIVERED while Recipient B's status remains IN_TRANSIT"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A legacy Shipment with one Recipient exists in the old schema","when":"The migration process runs","then":"The Shipment now has a Recipients collection with exactly one entry whose name, address, and phone match the original Recipient fields"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment is created","when":"An external partner API call is triggered","then":"The partner receives either N individual payloads (one per Recipient) in the existing v1 format OR a single v2 payload, depending on partner configuration—never a broken payload"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Shipment with one Recipient exists","when":"The user attempts to remove the last Recipient","then":"The operation is rejected with an error: a Shipment must have at least one Recipient"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"A Shipment is being created","when":"The user adds two Recipients with the same address","then":"The operation is rejected with a validation error indicating duplicate addresses"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment exists","when":"The courier views the delivery route","then":"Each Recipient appears as a separate stop with their individual delivery preferences displayed"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A user creates a Shipment and then removes all Recipients before adding new ones","expected_behavior":"The system must prevent the intermediate state of zero Recipients. Either enforce atomic replacement or block removal of the last Recipient."},{"scenario":"Two Recipients on the same Shipment are at adjacent addresses serviced by the same courier stop","expected_behavior":"They remain distinct Recipients with separate delivery confirmations, even if the courier physically delivers them together. Operational optimization does not merge domain entities."},{"scenario":"An external partner has not yet upgraded to support multi-recipient payloads","expected_behavior":"The integration layer projects the Shipment as N individual v1 calls, one per Recipient, maintaining backward compatibility."},{"scenario":"A legacy Shipment is accessed via API before the migration job reaches it (lazy vs. batch migration)","expected_behavior":"The API layer must handle both old and new schemas gracefully—either via a read-time transformation or by ensuring migration completes before the new model goes live."},{"scenario":"A Recipient's phone number or address is updated mid-delivery on a multi-recipient Shipment","expected_behavior":"Only that specific Recipient's delivery details are updated. Other Recipients and their in-flight delivery statuses are unaffected."},{"scenario":"A multi-recipient Shipment where one Recipient refuses delivery","expected_behavior":"That Recipient is marked REFUSED. The Shipment remains active for other Recipients. Shipment is only fully resolved when all Recipients have a terminal status (DELIVERED, REFUSED, FAILED, etc.)."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Multi-recipient shipments could increase revenue per shipment by consolidating what were previously separate orders. However, split-delivery routing increases operational cost. Pricing model must evolve—charging per Recipient or per Shipment has significant margin implications.","operational":"Major operational complexity increase: route optimization now involves multiple stops per Shipment, partial delivery states require new courier workflows, and customer support must handle per-Recipient inquiries on a single tracking number. Data migration of ~2M records is a significant effort requiring downtime planning or dual-read capability.","user":"End users gain flexibility to ship to multiple people in one transaction. However, tracking UX becomes more complex—a single Shipment now has multiple delivery statuses. Clear per-Recipient tracking views are essential to avoid confusion.","risk":"HIGH. This touches the core domain model. Risks include: (1) data migration errors on 2M records, (2) external partner API breakage if backward compatibility fails, (3) partial delivery state bugs leading to lost packages, (4) pricing/billing errors if not updated to account for multi-recipient costs.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"End Customers (Senders)","impact":"New capability to send to multiple recipients in one shipment. Must understand new tracking model."},{"group":"Recipients","impact":"No direct change to their experience if delivery preferences work correctly per-recipient."},{"group":"Couriers","impact":"Route planning changes significantly. A single shipment may now require multiple stops. Training and tooling updates needed."},{"group":"External Partners","impact":"API contract changes. Must either support v2 payloads or receive multiple v1 payloads. Integration testing required."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Must handle per-recipient status inquiries. Training on new partial-delivery states. Tooling must show per-recipient detail."},{"group":"Finance/Billing","impact":"Pricing model must be re-evaluated. Per-recipient vs per-shipment billing affects revenue and customer expectations."},{"group":"Engineering/Data","impact":"Schema migration, API versioning, backward compatibility layer, new status state machine. High implementation effort."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The product team's request is clear in intent but vague in critical details. 'Delivery preferences' is undefined—we don't know what preferences are supported. The interaction model between multi-recipient shipments and existing partner APIs is unspecified. The pricing and billing implications are not addressed. The migration strategy (lazy vs. batch, downtime vs. zero-downtime) is unstated. These gaps create ambiguity across multiple dimensions.","high_confidence_areas":["The core invariant that a Shipment must have >= 1 Recipient","The need for per-Recipient delivery status tracking","Backward compatibility requirement for external partner APIs","Data migration necessity for ~2M existing records"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact definition of 'delivery preferences' — what options are supported?","Pricing model: per-shipment or per-recipient billing?","Migration strategy: batch, lazy, or dual-schema?","Maximum number of Recipients per Shipment — is there an upper bound?","Whether multi-recipient means multi-address or could include same-address scenarios (e.g., multiple people at one office)"],"assumptions":["Delivery preferences include at minimum: time windows, signature requirements, and safe-place authorization","Each Recipient has a distinct address (same-address Recipients are not a valid use case)","External partners are not yet aware of this change and backward compatibility is mandatory","The Shipment aggregate boundary expands to own the Recipients collection"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"'Delivery preferences' is referenced but never defined. The spec says 'each Recipient can have different delivery preferences' without specifying what preferences exist. This affects schema design, UI, courier workflows, and partner API payloads.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Couriers","End Customers"],"options":["A) Define a minimal set: time window, signature required, safe-place authorized","B) Make preferences a flexible key-value store extensible over time","C) Defer preferences to a follow-up iteration and ship multi-recipient with uniform delivery first"],"recommendation":"Option A — define a concrete minimal set now. Flexible schemas tend to produce inconsistent data. Ship with a known set, extend later."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No upper bound specified for Recipients per Shipment. Is it 2? 10? 100? This has massive implications for routing, UI design, courier workload, and billing.","affected_stakeholders":["Couriers","Engineering","Finance","End Customers"],"options":["A) No limit — treat as unbounded collection","B) Set a practical upper bound (e.g., 10 Recipients per Shipment)","C) Tier limits based on customer plan or shipment type"],"recommendation":"Option B — set a reasonable upper bound (e.g., 5-10). Unbounded collections create operational nightmares for couriers and degrade UX."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Pricing model for multi-recipient Shipments is unspecified. A Shipment with 5 Recipients costs significantly more to deliver than one with 1 Recipient, but the billing model is not addressed.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","End Customers","Product"],"options":["A) Charge per Recipient (base + per-stop fee)","B) Charge per Shipment with volume tiers","C) Charge per Shipment with distance-based surcharges"],"recommendation":"Option A — per-Recipient pricing most accurately reflects operational cost and is simplest for customers to understand."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Migration strategy for ~2M existing Shipments is unspecified. Batch migration risks downtime; lazy migration risks dual-schema bugs; dual-write adds complexity.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","End Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["A) Batch migration with scheduled maintenance window","B) Lazy migration on read/write with compatibility layer","C) Dual-write period with eventual cutover"],"recommendation":"Option A if a maintenance window is acceptable, otherwise Option B with thorough read-path testing. The key invariant is that no Shipment data is lost or corrupted during migration."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
